incident_description.description_of_incident:
On 2025-11-29, at approximately 06:40 local time, our company-operated tug and barge unit experienced a minor fuel oil release while maneuvering within the Seattle Harbor turning basin, roughly 0.5 nautical miles south of Pier 36.

I, Maria Lopez, am the Port Operations Manager for Pacific Harbor Lines, Inc. I was on duty and in the operations office when the incident occurred, monitoring vessel movements over VHF and AIS. The tug M/V PACIFIC RESOLUTE (a 82-foot harbor tug) was assisting our fuel barge PHL-207 (double-hulled) during a routine repositioning from our fueling dock to a lay berth. Weather at the time was calm with overcast skies, light rain, and very light winds from the south (estimated 3–5 knots). Visibility was good at about 4–5 miles.

During the maneuver, the tug’s port-side day tank low-level alarm had recently been cleared after a controlled fuel transfer from the main storage tanks. As the vessel came astern and made a slight port turn to line up with the berth, the chief engineer noted an irregular reading on the port main engine fuel pressure gauge and a faint diesel odor on deck. Almost simultaneously, the deckhand on the stern reported seeing a slight sheen forming on the water surface near the port quarter of the tug, extending slowly toward the barge.

The officer in charge (the tug master) immediately ordered the engineer to secure the port main engine and switched propulsion to the starboard engine only. The engineer then inspected the engine room and quickly identified that a flexible fuel return line on the port main engine had developed a small split near a hose clamp, likely due to age and vibration fatigue. This allowed marine diesel fuel from the return system to spray intermittently onto a warm engine surface and down into the bilge area. The bilge, which had a small amount of residual fuel-water mixture from prior operations, was being managed by an automatic bilge pump. At the time of the failure, the bilge pump cycled on and discharged a small quantity of this contaminated bilge water overboard through the port-side discharge outlet before the crew recognized the issue.

As soon as the sheen was reported, the master directed the deckhand to deploy a pre-staged sorbent boom and absorbent pads from the tug’s spill kit. The crew laid approximately 75 feet of sorbent boom in a U-shape around the visible sheen, which at its maximum was estimated at roughly 30 feet by 15 feet in size. The sheen was thin and rainbow-colored, consistent with a small volume of diesel on the surface. Based on the engineer’s calculations and subsequent checks of tank soundings and bilge levels, we estimate that no more than 2–3 gallons of diesel fuel entered the water, with the majority of the material remaining contained within the bilge and subsequently pumped into dedicated waste containers once the automatic bilge system was disabled.

I was notified by VHF radio at approximately 06:45 that a sheen was present. I instructed the master to cease all non-essential operations, maintain position using minimal propulsion to avoid spreading the sheen, and continue containment efforts. I also directed the engineer to immediately secure the automatic bilge pump system and isolate the affected bilge compartment. The crew placed additional absorbent pads around the bilge discharge outlet and verified that no further discharges were occurring.

Within roughly 10–15 minutes, the crew had fully encircled the affected area with sorbent boom and had deployed enough absorbent pads to capture the bulk of the visible sheen. The water conditions were calm enough that the boom remained effective, and there was no evidence of the sheen migrating beyond our immediate operating area near the berth. No wildlife (such as seabirds, seals, or fish) were observed to be oiled or in distress at any point. The incident took place entirely within an active commercial harbor area that is regularly used for tug and barge operations.

As part of our internal procedures, I documented the crew’s actions and took down times, locations, and names of the personnel involved. The master, chief engineer, and deckhand all provided verbal statements to me within an hour of the incident, and I recorded their accounts in our incident log. We also took photographs from the tug showing the extent of the sheen, the deployed boom, and the damaged fuel hose segment after it was removed.

As an immediate corrective action, the damaged flexible fuel return hose was isolated and removed from service. A new hose section, meeting current manufacturer and class requirements, was installed later that same day after the engine was fully cooled and inspected. The engineer also manually cleaned the affected bilge section using absorbent pads and transferred all contaminated materials—including used pads, the damaged hose, and the captured bilge mixture—into properly labeled, closed containers for later disposal by a licensed waste management contractor.

In response to this incident, we initiated a broader review of all flexible fuel lines and bilge pumping arrangements across our harbor tug fleet. We have already scheduled an accelerated inspection and replacement program for fuel hoses showing signs of wear, chafing, or age-related stiffness. Additionally, we are revising our engineering standing orders to require that automatic bilge pumps in machinery spaces be placed in manual mode whenever fuel system maintenance or adjustments are ongoing, or immediately following any abnormal fuel system indication, so that any potentially contaminated bilge water is not discharged overboard without a specific visual check.

No injuries occurred as a result of this incident, and there were no collisions or groundings. The barge PHL-207 remained secure, and no cargo (fuel product) was released from the barge itself. The only material involved was marine diesel fuel associated with the tug’s own propulsion system. The harbor remained open to traffic, and there was no need for outside emergency response beyond our own crew’s initial actions. However, in keeping with our regulatory obligations and company policy, we are reporting this event in detail so that it is formally documented and can be reviewed by the appropriate agencies.

We recognize that even a small quantity of fuel in the water is a serious matter, particularly in a busy harbor environment. We are treating this as a preventable equipment failure and are implementing the corrective and preventive measures described above to reduce the likelihood of recurrence and to further tighten our controls over bilge management and fuel system integrity.
incident_description.occurred_discovered_planned: Occurred and discovered on 2025-11-29 at approximately 06:40 local time during harbor maneuvering operations.
